ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tomato-based sauces; used as the closest defensible anchor for this tomato-and-pepper dip. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Acili Ezme.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Acili Ezme is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a side dip alongside flatbread or crudités in a Middle Eastern or Turkish meal setting.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tomato-based dip / salsa composite; adapted with red pepper paste and olive oil component adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Acili Ezme. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for raw tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), red bell pepper (FDC ID 170108), onion (FDC ID 170000), garlic (FDC ID 169230), and olive oil (FDC ID 171413), weighted to reflect the typical recipe composition of this dip. The input record's per_100g values (energy 120 kcal, protein 2.33 g, fat 8.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are consistent with a tomato-and-olive-oil-based dip and are retained as the best available estimate. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g reflects added salt and red pepper paste contribution. Calcium 26.67 mg, iron 1.0 mg, and vitamin C 20 mg are plausible for this vegetable-based preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on tomato-based salsa/dip (GI ~35) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Acili Ezme. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy to tomato-based salsa and vegetable dips reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The dip is predominantly tomatoes, red pepper, and olive oil with low net carbohydrate content (10 g/100 g) and significant fibre (2.67 g/100 g) and fat (8 g/100 g), both of which attenuate glycaemic response. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.2) / 100 ≈ 5.3 (available carbohydrate = 10.0 − 2.67 = 7.33 g/100 g × 1.5 serving = 11.0 g; GL = 35 × 11.0 / 100 = 3.85, rounded to 5.3 using gross carbohydrate for conservatism: 10.0 × 1.5 = 15.0 g; GL = 35 × 15.0 / 100 = 5.25 ≈ 5.3).

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g (450 mg per 150 g serving) approaches one-fifth of the 2,000 mg daily limit; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 75–100 g, while the potassium-rich tomatoes, onions, and garlic provide partial offsetting benefit consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (~35) with moderate carbohydrate (10 g/100 g) and fibre (2.67 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 35 and a glycaemic load of approximately 5.3 per 150 g serving, Acili Ezme is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, pomegranate molasses contributes natural and potentially added sugars, so requesting reduced sauce and pairing with wholemeal bread rather than white bread will further minimise postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat from olive oil; saturated fat 1.0 g/100 g; zero trans fat; antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile is dominated by olive oil monounsaturated fatty acids with saturated fat at only 1.0 g/100 g and confirmed zero trans fat, making this dip cardioprotective in moderate portions; the main cardiovascular caution is sodium at 300 mg/100 g, which should be reduced in preparation for individuals on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory olive oil and pomegranate molasses; nightshade ingredients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Olive oil (oleocanthal), garlic (allicin), and pomegranate molasses (ellagic acid) provide anti-inflammatory benefit; however, tomatoes, red pepper paste, and chilli are nightshade ingredients and individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor symptom response and consider requesting a reduced-chilli preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Acili Ezme (Turkish: Acılı Ezme, meaning 'spicy mash' or 'spicy paste') is a traditional Turkish cold dip and relish made by finely chopping or pulsing ripe tomatoes, red pepper paste (biber salçası), onions, garlic, and fresh parsley, then dressing with olive oil, pomegranate molasses, lemon juice, red pepper flakes, and salt. The result is a vibrant, tangy, moderately spicy condiment with a chunky-to-smooth texture depending on preparation style. It is served chilled as a meze alongside flatbread, grilled meats, or shawarma, and is a staple of Turkish and broader Middle Eastern restaurant menus. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 120 kcal, 2.33 g protein, 8.0 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil, saturated fat only 1.0 g), 10.0 g carbohydrate, 5.33 g sugar, 2.67 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. Vitamin C at 20 mg/100 g reflects the tomato and pepper content. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The high sodium content (300 mg/100 g) is the primary clinical caution for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Middle Eastern and Turkish restaurants and shawarma outlets.
common_user_queries: Is acili ezme suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in acili ezme?
What is in Turkish spicy tomato dip?
Is acili ezme suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Acili Ezme exists in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component foods weighted to a typical recipe composition, and the input record's values are consistent with this estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using tomato-based salsa as the closest approved comparator. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this prepared Turkish dip is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16; the fat is predominantly olive oil and is expected to be largely monounsaturated. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not available in approved sources; estimated ~280 mg/100 g from tomatoes and onions but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not populated. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this preparation not reported in approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail for this composite dip not available in approved sources as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — olive-oil-based preparation contains no industrially produced trans fats; consistent with USDA data for olive oil and raw vegetable components). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based). The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which reflects the point of sale (GrabFood Singapore listing); the true culinary origin is Turkey and this is reflected in region_of_origin and cuisine fields. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not constitute an approved nutrient source and has not been used for nutrient values.
